section.title: The Catch of Fish and the Call of Levi
section.sectionRationale: Having announced His mission, Jesus now begins to gather those who will carry it. A miraculous catch of fish exposes Simon Peter's sin and calls him to catch men; a leper is cleansed by a touch; a paralytic is forgiven and healed, revealing the Son of Man's authority to forgive; and a tax collector named Levi leaves his booth to follow. Around a table of sinners Jesus declares that He came not for the righteous but to call sinners to repentance, and the parables of the patch and the wineskins signal that something wholly new has arrived.
section.thesis: Jesus calls sinners, not the self-sufficient, drawing fishermen, lepers, the paralyzed, and tax collectors to Himself, forgiving sins and healing bodies with divine authority, and announcing that the new thing He brings cannot be sewn onto the old.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the miraculous catch and the call of Simon, James, and John; the cleansing of a leper; the forgiveness and healing of a paralytic; the call of Levi and the meal with sinners; and the parables of the patch and the wineskins.

units.label: Put out into deep water
units.note: from now on you will catch men

verses.text: On one occasion, while Jesus was standing by the Lake of Gennesaret with the crowd pressing in on Him to hear the word of God,
He saw two boats at the edge of the lake. The fishermen had left them and were washing their nets.
Jesus got into the boat belonging to Simon and asked him to put out a little from shore. And sitting down, He taught the people from the boat.
When Jesus had finished speaking, He said to Simon, “Put out into deep water and let down your nets for a catch.”
“Master,” Simon replied, “we have worked hard all night without catching anything. But because You say so, I will let down the nets.”
When they had done so, they caught such a large number of fish that their nets began to tear.
So they signaled to their partners in the other boat to come and help them, and they came and filled both boats so full that they began to sink.
When Simon Peter saw this, he fell at Jesus’ knees. “Go away from me, Lord,” he said, “for I am a sinful man.”
For he and his companions were astonished at the catch of fish they had taken,
and so were his partners James and John, the sons of Zebedee. “Do not be afraid,” Jesus said to Simon. “From now on you will catch men.”
And when they had brought their boats ashore, they left everything and {{akoloutheo|followed}} Him.
While Jesus was in one of the towns, a man came along who was covered with leprosy. When he saw Jesus, he fell facedown and begged Him, “Lord, if You are willing, You can make me clean.”
Jesus reached out His hand and touched the man. “I am willing,” He said. “Be clean!” And immediately the leprosy left him.
“Do not tell anyone,” Jesus instructed him. “But go, show yourself to the priest and present the offering Moses prescribed for your cleansing, as a testimony to them.”
But the news about Jesus spread all the more, and great crowds came to hear Him and to be healed of their sicknesses.
Yet He frequently withdrew to the wilderness to pray.

units.label: Your sins are forgiven
units.note: the Son of Man's authority to forgive

verses.text: One day Jesus was teaching, and the Pharisees and teachers of the law were sitting there. People had come from Jerusalem and from every village of Galilee and Judea, and the power of the Lord was present for Him to heal the sick.
Just then some men came carrying a paralyzed man on a mat. They tried to bring him inside to set him before Jesus,
but they could not find a way through the crowd. So they went up on the roof and lowered him on his mat through the tiles into the middle of the crowd, right in front of Jesus.
When Jesus saw their faith, He said, “Friend, your sins are {{aphiemi|forgiven}}.”
But the scribes and Pharisees began thinking to themselves, “Who is this man who speaks blasphemy? Who can forgive sins but God alone?”
Knowing what they were thinking, Jesus replied, “Why are you thinking these things in your hearts?
Which is easier: to say, ‘Your sins are forgiven,’ or to say, ‘Get up and walk’?
But so that you may know that the Son of Man has {{exousia|authority}} on the earth to forgive sins...” He said to the paralytic, “I tell you, get up, pick up your mat, and go home.”
And immediately the man stood up before them, took what he had been lying on, and went home glorifying God.
Everyone was taken with amazement and glorified God. They were filled with awe and said, “We have seen remarkable things today.”

units.label: The call of Levi
units.note: I have not come to call the righteous but sinners

verses.text: After this, Jesus went out and saw a tax collector named Levi sitting at the tax booth. “{{akoloutheo|Follow Me}},” He told him,
and Levi got up, left everything, and followed Him.
Then Levi hosted a great banquet for Jesus at his house. A large crowd of tax collectors was there, along with others who were eating with them.
But the Pharisees and their scribes complained to Jesus’ disciples, “Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?”
Jesus answered, “It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick.
I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners, to {{metanoia|repentance}}.”

units.label: New wine, new wineskins
units.note: the bridegroom and the newness of the kingdom

verses.text: Then they said to Him, “John’s disciples and those of the Pharisees frequently fast and pray, but Yours keep on eating and drinking.”
Jesus replied, “Can you make the guests of the bridegroom fast while He is with them?
But the time will come when the bridegroom will be taken from them; then they will fast.”

He also told them a parable: “No one tears a piece of cloth from a new garment and sews it on an old one. If he does, he will tear the new garment as well, and the patch from the new will not match the old.
And no one pours new wine into old wineskins. If he does, the new wine will burst the skins, the wine will spill, and the wineskins will be ruined.
Instead, new wine is poured into new wineskins.
And no one after drinking old wine wants new, for he says, ‘The old is better.’”

akoloutheo.gloss: To follow, to come after as a disciple. It means more than walking behind; it is attaching one's whole life to a master. The fishermen and Levi akoloutheo Jesus by leaving everything, the pattern of true discipleship throughout the Gospel.

aphiemi.gloss: To let go, release, forgive, send away. It is the verb behind the noun for forgiveness. When Jesus tells the paralytic his sins are aphiemi, forgiven, He does what the scribes rightly say only God can do, and then heals the man to prove His authority to do it.

exousia.gloss: Authority, the rightful power to act. Jesus claims that the Son of Man has exousia on earth to forgive sins, and He backs the claim with a visible healing, showing that His authority reaches into the invisible realm of guilt and pardon.

metanoia.gloss: A change of mind that turns the whole life around; repentance. Jesus says He came to call sinners to metanoia, not to affirm the self-righteous. The tax collectors and sinners at Levi's table are exactly the people His call is for.

basic.context: After announcing His mission, Jesus begins to gather followers. He tells the tired fisherman Simon to let down his nets, and the overwhelming catch drives Simon to his knees as a sinner; Jesus calls him to catch men. Jesus cleanses a leper with a touch, forgives and heals a paralytic lowered through a roof, and calls a tax collector named Levi, then feasts with sinners. When criticized, He says He came for the sick, not the healthy, and tells parables of a patch and wineskins to show that His kingdom is something new.
basic.hermeneutics: Read these scenes together as a portrait of who Jesus calls and how. The common thread is grace to the unlikely: sinful fishermen, an untouchable leper, a helpless paralytic, a despised tax collector. The forgiveness of the paralytic is the theological center, where Jesus claims divine authority. The wineskins parable interprets the whole: the new wine of the kingdom cannot simply be poured into the old forms.
basic.originalLanguages: The fishermen and Levi akoloutheo Jesus, follow Him, by leaving everything. Jesus tells the paralytic his sins are aphiemi, forgiven, doing what only God can do, and proves He has exousia, authority, to forgive by healing him. And He declares He came to call sinners to metanoia, repentance, the turning that the proud think they do not need.
basic.typology: The cleansing of the leper by touch, without Jesus being defiled, points to the One who takes our uncleanness and makes us clean. The forgiveness of the paralytic reveals the Son of Man of Daniel 7, given authority and dominion. The bridegroom image casts Jesus as the long-promised husband of His people, whose presence is cause for joy, not fasting.
basic.christ: Jesus is Lord over creation, commanding the fish; He is holy, so that Simon feels his sin in His presence; He is the compassionate healer who touches the untouchable; He is the Son of Man with authority to forgive; and He is the friend of sinners and the bridegroom of His people. In every scene He draws the unworthy to Himself and makes them His own.
basic.god: God's power is present in Jesus to heal and, more wonderfully, to forgive, the prerogative the scribes rightly reserve to God alone. God calls not the self-righteous but sinners, welcoming tax collectors to the table. And God is doing a genuinely new thing in Jesus that cannot be contained by the old religious forms.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 6 shows the same holy fear before God that Simon feels. Leviticus 13 and 14 lie behind the leper's cleansing and the priestly testimony. Daniel 7:13-14 names the Son of Man given authority. 1 Timothy 1:15 sums it up: Christ came to save sinners.
basic.oneStory: The Savior who announced good news to the poor now gathers a people of the unlikely. He calls sinful fishermen, cleanses a leper, forgives a paralytic with divine authority, and seats a tax collector at His table, declaring that He came for sinners. The new wine of His kingdom is bursting the old skins; the age of the bridegroom has arrived, and He is drawing the guilty and the outcast into the joy of following Him.

context.literary: This chapter is a gallery of call and controversy. It opens with a miracle that becomes a call, moves through two healings that reveal Jesus' authority, adds the call of Levi and the feast with sinners, and closes with paired parables that interpret the newness of it all. Luke pairs healing with word: the leper and the paralytic show Jesus' power, while the sayings about the physician, the bridegroom, and the wineskins give the meaning.
context.historical: The Lake of Gennesaret is the Sea of Galilee, where night fishing with nets was common. Leprosy made a person ritually unclean and socially cut off, so a touch was startling. Roofs of packed earth on beams could be opened, as the paralytic's friends did. Tax collectors like Levi gathered tolls for Rome and were despised as traitors and cheats, which is why sharing a table with them scandalized the Pharisees.
context.canonical: The call to catch men echoes the mission that will fill Acts. The Son of Man's authority to forgive draws on Daniel 7 and anticipates the cross where that forgiveness is secured. The bridegroom saying joins a rich biblical theme of God as husband to His people, Hosea and Isaiah, fulfilled in Christ and consummated in Revelation 19. The new wineskins announce the new covenant.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show whom Jesus calls and on what basis, sinners drawn by grace rather than the righteous relying on themselves, to reveal His divine authority to forgive as well as to heal, and to signal through the parables that the kingdom He brings is a new thing that cannot be confined to the old forms.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific events, a miraculous catch, particular healings, one man's call at a tax booth, describe how Jesus acted in history. The abiding truths are that Jesus calls sinners to follow, that He alone has authority to forgive, and that discipleship means leaving all to follow Him. The details of first-century fishing or roofing are setting, not command.

debates.question: Why did Jesus link forgiving the paralytic's sins with healing his body?

views.label: The healing proves the authority to forgive
views.case: Anyone can claim to forgive sins, since no one can see whether it happened; by commanding the visible healing, Jesus gives public proof that He also holds the invisible authority to forgive, the very thing the scribes reserve to God.

views.label: Sin and its effects are addressed together
views.case: While not every illness is tied to a specific sin, sickness and death entered the world through the fall; in healing the body and forgiving the soul, Jesus shows the whole scope of the salvation He brings.

debates.bearing: Either way the point stands: the Son of Man openly claims and demonstrates a divine authority, forcing the question of who He really is.

debates.question: What do the parables of the patch and the wineskins mean?

views.label: The newness of the kingdom cannot be tied to the old forms
views.case: The gospel Jesus brings is not a repair or a patch on the old religious system; it is a new thing that needs new structures, so trying to contain it within legalistic forms only ruins both.

views.label: A warning against clinging to the familiar
views.case: The closing line, the old is better, exposes the human preference for the accustomed; those comfortable with the old wine of self-righteous religion resist the new wine of grace.

debates.bearing: Both readings press the hearer to receive the new work of God in Christ rather than forcing it into old, self-made categories or preferring the comfortable past.

study.originalLanguages: Discipleship in this chapter is captured by akoloutheo, to follow: the fishermen and Levi leave everything to come after Jesus. Its heart is the scene with the paralytic, where Jesus declares the man's sins aphiemi, forgiven, the verb behind the word for forgiveness, doing what the scribes rightly say only God can do. To prove He holds exousia, authority, on earth to forgive, He heals the man before their eyes. And at Levi's table Jesus states His mission plainly: He came to call sinners to metanoia, the repentance that turns the whole life around, the very thing the self-satisfied assume they do not need. The parables of patch and wineskins then declare that this is new wine, not a patch on the old.

typology.type: The cleansing of the leper by touch
typology.fulfillment: Jesus takes the uncleanness upon Himself without being defiled, foreshadowing the One who bears our sin and makes us clean, 2 Corinthians 5:21.

typology.type: The Son of Man with authority, Daniel 7
typology.fulfillment: Jesus claims the authority to forgive sins on earth, the dominion given to the Son of Man, secured at the cross.
Jesus is the promised husband of God's people, whose coming turns fasting to feasting, consummated at the marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19.

study.christ: Every scene reveals Jesus. He is Lord of creation, filling the nets; He is the Holy One, before whom Simon confesses his sin; He is the compassionate healer who touches the leper others shunned; He is the Son of Man with divine authority to forgive; and He is the bridegroom and friend of sinners who calls a tax collector from his booth and feasts with the outcast. He does not wait for people to make themselves worthy; He calls sinners, forgives them, and makes them followers, and the new wine of His grace bursts every old and narrow form.
study.god: Here the power of the Lord is present to heal, and the deeper wonder is that Jesus forgives, exercising what Israel knew belonged to God alone. God's heart is revealed in whom Jesus seeks: not the self-assured righteous but the sick who know their need, tax collectors and sinners welcomed to the table. And God is shown doing a genuinely new thing, the dawning kingdom that cannot be sewn onto the old garment of self-righteous religion but calls for new hearts to receive it.

crossReferences.note: Isaiah cries Woe is me, I am ruined, before the holy God, the same fear that drives Simon to his knees before Jesus.
The offering Moses prescribed for a cleansed leper, which Jesus tells the man to present as a testimony to the priests.
The Son of Man given authority and dominion, the title and prerogative Jesus claims in forgiving the paralytic.
Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, the plain sense of I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners.

study.oneStory: The Savior who proclaimed release now begins to gather the released. He calls sinful fishermen who leave their nets, cleanses a leper no one would touch, forgives a paralytic with the authority of God, and seats a despised tax collector at His table, announcing that He came for sinners, not the self-righteous. The bridegroom has arrived and the new wine of the kingdom is poured out, too full and living to be held by the old skins. This is the company Jesus is forming, the unlikely and the unworthy, called by grace to follow Him.

quiz.q: How did Simon Peter respond to the miraculous catch of fish?

options.text: He fell at Jesus' knees and confessed himself a sinful man.
options.note: In verse 8 the catch reveals Jesus' holiness and Simon's sin, driving him to his knees.

options.text: He was amazed and grateful for such a great haul.
options.note: He was astonished, but his deeper response was conviction of sin, not just gratitude for the fish.

options.text: He immediately asked Jesus for another miracle.
options.note: He did not seek more signs; he shrank back, aware of his unworthiness before Jesus.

options.text: He doubted that the catch had anything to do with Jesus.
options.note: The opposite: he saw Jesus as Lord and felt his own sinfulness in His presence.

quiz.q: What was remarkable about how Jesus healed the man covered with leprosy?

options.text: He reached out and touched the man, then declared Him clean, willingly bearing contact with the unclean.
options.note: Verse 13 shows Jesus touching the leper, a startling act of compassion and power.

options.text: He healed the man from a distance with a word.
options.note: Jesus did speak, but Luke stresses that He touched the untouchable man, which is the striking detail.

options.text: He sent the man to the priests to be healed there.
options.note: He sent the cleansed man to the priest as a testimony, but the healing came from Jesus' own touch and word.

options.text: He refused to go near the man because of his disease.
options.note: Far from refusing, Jesus deliberately reached out and touched him.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus forgive the paralytic's sins before healing him?

options.text: To show that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins, proven by the visible healing.
options.note: Verse 24 states His purpose: the healing demonstrates His authority to forgive.

options.text: Because the man's paralysis was directly caused by a specific sin.
options.note: Sin and suffering are related since the fall, but Jesus' point was His authority to forgive, not a one-to-one link.

options.text: To avoid having to heal the man at all.
options.note: He did heal him; the forgiveness and the healing together displayed His authority.

options.text: Because forgiveness is a lesser thing than healing.
options.note: Forgiveness is the greater gift; the healing was given to prove His right to forgive.

quiz.q: Whom did Jesus say He came to call?

options.text: Sinners to repentance, not the righteous, like a doctor who comes for the sick.
options.note: Verses 31 and 32 give His mission: He came for sinners, not the self-righteous.

options.text: The tax collectors and outcasts of society.
options.note: He does call such people, but His words name a wider principle: all who know they are sinners in need.

options.text: Those who had already made themselves righteous.
options.note: The opposite of His words; He says He did not come to call the righteous.

options.text: Only the religious leaders who kept the law.
options.note: He was criticized by those leaders precisely for calling sinners instead.

quiz.q: What do the parables of the patch and the new wineskins teach?

options.text: The new life of the kingdom Jesus brings cannot be confined to the old forms; it calls for something new.
options.note: Verses 36 to 38 show the new wine bursting old skins, a picture of the kingdom's newness.

options.text: That old traditions should always be discarded as worthless.
options.note: The point is the incompatibility of new and old forms, not that everything old is worthless; the old prepared for the new.

options.text: That Jesus came merely to patch up the existing religious system.
options.note: The parables say the opposite: His kingdom is not a patch on the old garment.

options.text: That fasting and prayer no longer matter at all.
options.note: Jesus says His followers will fast when the bridegroom is taken; the parables are about newness, not abolishing devotion.

questions: Simon obeyed Jesus even when it made no sense to his experience: 'because You say so, I will let down the nets.' Where is Jesus asking you to trust His word over your own judgment?
The catch of fish made Simon aware of his sin, yet Jesus said, 'Do not be afraid.' How does meeting the holiness of Jesus and His grace together change the way you come to Him?
Jesus touched the leper no one else would touch. Is there an area of shame or uncleanness in your life you fear to bring to Him, forgetting that He is willing?
The paralytic's friends carried him to Jesus and would not give up. Who are you carrying to Jesus in prayer, and whose faith once carried you?

Levi left everything at the tax booth and followed at once, then threw a feast so his friends could meet Jesus. What would leaving everything to follow look like for you, and who would you want at that table?

translationNotes.note: The Lake of Gennesaret is another name for the Sea of Galilee, as the BSB footnote notes. Luke, writing for a wider audience, uses the term lake rather than the more traditional sea.

Leprosy in Scripture covers a range of serious skin diseases, not only the condition called leprosy today, as the BSB footnote explains with reference to Leviticus 13. Such a person was ceremonially unclean and cut off from the community, which makes Jesus' touch all the more striking.

The offering Moses prescribed for a cleansed leper is set out in Leviticus 14:1-32. Presenting it to the priest served as a testimony that a true cleansing had taken place, a public witness to what Jesus had done.

Son of Man is Jesus' favored self-designation, drawing on Daniel 7:13-14, where one like a son of man is given authority and everlasting dominion. Here Jesus claims that authority extends to forgiving sins on earth, which the scribes rightly regard as God's prerogative alone.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 5:1-39. Headings, line breaks, poem and note markers, and footnotes were skipped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this chapter; none needed conversion. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon on Luke 5 from the trusted cluster was verified this session (the nearest Alistair Begg message on Levi's call is on Mark 2, not Luke 5).
